stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 presents for her 28-week antenatal visit at the community midwifery clinic. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated and her son is now 3 years old. The pregnancy has been well, with normal morphology scan, normal OGTT, and Hb 118 g/L. Her partner accompanies her today, as he has at every visit; he sits close, answers some of her questions for her, and at the last visit asked the midwife to keep him informed of all results. The woman is quieter than usual, makes minimal eye contact, and has a fading bruise on her left forearm which she attributes to a kitchen accident. Routine domestic violence screening is part of the antenatal pathway in this service. The clinic has a private examination room, a translator phone line, and a social worker available on site this afternoon.

data_table.Examination: SFH 28 cm, foetal heart 142, longitudinal lie, cephalic presentation; healing 4 cm bruise left forearm
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, MCV 84 fL, urinalysis NAD
data_table.Social: Lives with partner and 3-year-old son; partner attends all visits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step to enable safe domestic violence enquiry?

options.text: Ask the screening questions in the consult room with the partner present to model openness
options.rationale: Enquiry in the presence of the partner is unsafe; disclosure rates fall and post-disclosure retaliation risk rises. RANZCOG and NSW Health antenatal DV screening protocols require the woman to be alone (RANZCOG Statement C-Obs 57; NSW Health Domestic Violence Routine Screening).

options.text: Defer the screening enquiry until the postnatal home visit when the partner is less likely to attend
options.rationale: Pregnancy is a high-risk period for escalation; deferring screening misses the antenatal opportunity at which most Australian services formally enquire (RANZCOG C-Obs 57).

options.text: Find a clinical reason to see her alone and enquire privately using a validated framework
options.rationale: Australian antenatal DV protocols require private enquiry away from the partner using a structured tool (e.g. HARK, AAS, abbreviated WAST). Risk should be documented in a non-shared part of the record with warm referral to the on-site social worker, which is the standard pathway (RANZCOG C-Obs 57; NSW Health DV Routine Screening; 1800RESPECT clinician resources).

options.text: Avoid direct enquiry today and place a 1800RESPECT information leaflet inside her hand-held antenatal notes
options.rationale: Placing DV resources in shared notes risks discovery and retaliation; direct, private enquiry is the recommended approach when indicators are present (RANZCOG C-Obs 57; 1800RESPECT).

options.text: Contact NSW Police immediately because the forearm bruise constitutes evidence of an assault
options.rationale: Mandatory police reporting of adult DV without consent is not the Australian framework; the adult survivor controls disclosure unless a child is at immediate risk or jurisdictional mandatory reporting applies (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; 1800RESPECT).

explanation.summary: Safe DV enquiry requires the woman be alone, a validated framework be used, the disclosure be documented in a non-shared place, and immediate referral pathways be activated. Antenatal pregnancy is a peak-risk window for escalation (RANZCOG C-Obs 57; NSW Health DV Routine Screening).
key_points: Never enquire with the partner present (RANZCOG C-Obs 57)
Use validated tool: HARK, AAS, or abbreviated WAST (RANZCOG C-Obs 57)
Document outside shared/hand-held notes (NSW Health)
Warm referral to 1800RESPECT and on-site social worker (1800RESPECT)
Pregnancy is a peak risk window for first-time and escalation DV (RANZCOG C-Obs 57)
explanation.pearls: If the partner refuses to leave, use a urine sample request or imaging excuse to create a private window (NSW Health).

subtopic_detail: Domestic violence screening - safe enquiry

tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health
stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 Aboriginal woman from a regional NSW community books at 11 weeks for antenatal care at the local hospital. She has no medical conditions, takes pregnancy multivitamins, does not smoke, and her BMI is 24. Her partner is supportive and she lives with her mother and two cousins. She tells the booking midwife she felt uncomfortable at a previous gynaecology appointment because she did not understand what was being said and felt unable to ask questions; she has skipped two follow-up appointments at other services in the past for the same reason. The hospital has both a standard midwifery group practice and a local Aboriginal Maternal and Infant Health Service (AMIHS) clinic that provides continuity-of-carer with an Aboriginal Health Worker and midwife, supports family involvement, and links with the regional ACCHO. The morphology ultrasound, OGTT, and specialist obstetric review can be provided through either pathway.

data_table.Booking bloods: Hb 122 g/L, blood group O Rh+, rubella immune, syphilis EIA non-reactive, HIV negative, HBsAg negative
data_table.Social: Lives with mother and cousins; supportive partner; previous disengagement from non-culturally-safe services

lead_in: Which model of antenatal care is most likely to optimise engagement and outcomes for this woman?

options.text: Standard midwifery group practice with referral to a separate Aboriginal Health Worker as needed
options.rationale: Parallel-but-separate models have been shown in Australian evaluations to be less effective than integrated continuity models such as AMIHS for engagement, attendance, and birth outcomes for Aboriginal women (NSW Health AMIHS Evaluation; Closing the Gap).

options.text: AMIHS continuity-of-carer model integrating midwife and Aboriginal Health Worker, with ACCHO linkage
options.rationale: AMIHS and analogous Birthing on Country / Bubup-Wilam style models are the Australian-evidence-supported pathway; they improve antenatal attendance, reduce preterm birth, increase breastfeeding rates and improve cultural safety for Aboriginal mothers and infants (NSW Health AMIHS Evaluation; RANZCOG Statement W-WH 22 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women; NACCHO).

options.text: Obstetrician-led care because all Aboriginal pregnancies are inherently high risk
options.rationale: Aboriginal identity is not itself a clinical risk factor; defaulting to specialist-led care reflects deficit framing rather than the actual evidence base. Risk is assessed individually using the same antenatal risk stratification (RANZCOG W-WH 22).

options.text: GP shared-care with the regional GP because this minimises hospital contact
options.rationale: GP shared care is a reasonable option for many women but does not address the prior disengagement or provide the integrated Aboriginal Health Worker continuity associated with improved outcomes for Aboriginal women (RANZCOG W-WH 22; NACCHO).

options.text: Telehealth-only antenatal care to reduce travel barriers regardless of preference
options.rationale: Telehealth supplements but does not replace face-to-face cultural-safety continuity; defaulting to telehealth without offering AMIHS misses the proven engagement model (RANZCOG W-WH 22).

explanation.summary: AMIHS / Birthing on Country / Bubup-Wilam type continuity-of-carer models combining midwife and Aboriginal Health Worker, with ACCHO linkage, have Australian evidence for improved antenatal attendance, reduced preterm birth, increased breastfeeding, and improved cultural safety (NSW Health AMIHS Evaluation; RANZCOG W-WH 22; NACCHO).
key_points: AMIHS = NSW model; analogous services exist in Vic (Koori Maternity), Qld (Birthing in Our Community)
Continuity-of-carer is the active ingredient (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
Aboriginal identity is not itself a risk factor (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
Closing the Gap target 2 measures healthy birthweight (Closing the Gap)
Offer choice; do not force into Aboriginal-specific service if the woman prefers standard care
explanation.pearls: Birthing on Country is a movement returning birthing services to community control; it is not a single model but a principle (NACCHO).

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander antenatal care - culturally safe model

subtopic: Indigenous health (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander)
tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health
stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 Aboriginal woman from a remote NT community presents for booking at 12 weeks. She has a documented history of acute rheumatic fever aged 10 with subsequent moderate mitral regurgitation on echo last year and is on monthly intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin (BPG) 1.2 million units secondary prophylaxis through the local clinic. Her last dose was 2 weeks ago. She has had one previous uncomplicated SVD at term with no cardiac decompensation, breastfed for 12 months, and is currently well with no exertional dyspnoea, no orthopnoea, and NYHA class I. Examination: BP 108/64, HR 82, soft pansystolic murmur at apex unchanged from previous, no peripheral oedema, clear chest. Repeat booking echo shows unchanged moderate MR, mild LA dilation, normal LV function, and pulmonary artery pressure 28 mmHg. She asks whether she should continue the monthly injections during pregnancy because a friend told her to stop because the penicillin might harm the baby.

data_table.Examination: Soft PSM apex unchanged; no signs of failure; SFH appropriate; foetal heart 156
data_table.Investigations: Echo: moderate MR, mild LA dilation, normal LVEF 60%, PASP 28 mmHg; Hb 120 g/L; ECG sinus
data_table.Medications: Benzathine benzylpenicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 28 days; pregnancy multivitamin

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding her secondary prophylaxis during pregnancy?

options.text: Continue 4-weekly IM BPG at the standard dose throughout pregnancy and the postpartum period
options.rationale: Benzathine benzylpenicillin does not cross the placenta in clinically meaningful amounts and is safe and recommended throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding; the dosing interval is unchanged. Multidisciplinary cardiac-obstetric review is required for any rheumatic heart disease in pregnancy (RHDAustralia Guideline; eTG Antibiotic; RANZCOG; NACCHO).

options.text: Switch to oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 250 mg BD for the duration of pregnancy to avoid IM injections
options.rationale: Oral prophylaxis has higher failure rates due to adherence and absorption; IM BPG is preferred for ARF/RHD secondary prophylaxis in Australia, including in pregnancy (RHDAustralia Guideline).

options.text: Cease benzathine benzylpenicillin prophylaxis until after delivery because penicillin readily crosses the placenta
options.rationale: Penicillin is safe across all trimesters; cessation risks ARF recurrence with permanent worsening of valvular damage and increased maternal mortality (RHDAustralia Guideline; AMH).

options.text: Shorten the BPG interval to every 3 weeks because pregnancy increases drug clearance and lowers troughs
options.rationale: While pharmacokinetic changes occur, the standard 4-weekly interval is retained for routine prophylaxis; 3-weekly is reserved for documented breakthrough ARF or selected high-risk situations as per specialist advice, not pregnancy alone (RHDAustralia Guideline).

options.text: Convert to azithromycin 1 g monthly as an alternative because penicillin allergy is more common in pregnancy
options.rationale: Macrolides are only used for documented penicillin allergy; pregnancy does not cause allergy, and azithromycin monthly is not the standard substitution (RHDAustralia Guideline).

explanation.summary: Continuation of 4-weekly IM BPG through pregnancy and lactation is the standard of care; multidisciplinary cardiac-obstetric review is recommended for moderate or severe RHD (RHDAustralia Guideline; RANZCOG W-WH 22).
key_points: BPG is Pregnancy Category A and safe in lactation (AMH)
Cessation risks ARF recurrence and progressive valve damage (RHDAustralia)
Moderate/severe RHD requires combined obstetric-cardiac team (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
Echocardiography baseline + each trimester for moderate or severe disease (RHDAustralia)
Anaesthetic and intrapartum planning at 28-32 weeks (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
explanation.pearls: ARF/RHD remains a leading cardiac cause of maternal morbidity in Aboriginal women; antenatal interruption of prophylaxis is a recurring contributor to adverse outcome (RHDAustralia).

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health - RHD secondary prophylaxis in pregnancy

subtopic: Indigenous health (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander)
tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health
stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 Aboriginal woman from a remote community, with known severe rheumatic mitral stenosis (MVA 1.0 cm2, mean gradient 14 mmHg, PASP 56 mmHg on third-trimester echo, NYHA II antenatally) presents to a regional referral centre at 39+2 weeks in spontaneous labour. She has been on monthly BPG, metoprolol 50 mg BD, and prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg daily, ceased 18 hours ago. She is 6 cm dilated, contracting 4-in-10, the foetus is cephalic with reassuring CTG, and an epidural has been sited and is providing good analgesia. Over the last 30 minutes she has become increasingly breathless, oxygen saturation has fallen from 98% to 91% on room air, HR has risen from 96 to 132 (sinus tachycardia on ECG with no shortened diastole arrhythmia and no fibrillatory waves), BP is 105/68, JVP is elevated, and she has bibasal crackles. Chest x-ray shows pulmonary venous congestion with no focal consolidation. Bedside echo confirms unchanged severe MS with no left atrial thrombus and preserved LV function. The cardiac-obstetric-anaesthetic team is gathering at the bedside, blood is cross-matched, and a high-dependency bed is available. Foetal heart rate remains 145 with normal variability and accelerations, and the obstetric assessment is that vaginal birth with assisted second stage remains feasible if she stabilises within minutes She has been counselled antenatally about the haemodynamic risks of tachycardia in severe mitral stenosis and the rationale for assisted second stage to avoid prolonged Valsalva.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 105/68, HR 132 sinus, RR 28, SpO2 91% RA -> 95% on 4 L NP, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: JVP +4 cm, bibasal crackles, loud P2, mid-diastolic rumble apex; gravid uterus contracting 4-in-10
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachy, no AF; CXR pulmonary congestion; ABG pH 7.40, pO2 78 mmHg on O2, lactate 1.8; troponin negative
data_table.CTG: Baseline 145, variability normal, accels present, no decels
data_table.Medications: Metoprolol 50 mg BD, BPG monthly, enoxaparin 40 mg ceased 18 h ago, epidural sited

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention to stabilise her acutely?

options.text: IV furosemide plus additional IV beta-blocker to lower heart rate and reduce filling pressures
options.rationale: In severe MS the haemodynamic enemy is tachycardia (shortened diastole, raised LA pressure, pulmonary oedema). Lowering rate with beta-blockade and offloading with a loop diuretic is the cornerstone of acute management while planning an assisted second stage to limit Valsalva effort. Emergency caesarean and balloon valvuloplasty are only required if she fails to stabilise (RANZCOG; ESC Pregnancy Cardiovascular Disease Guideline).

options.text: Tocolyse with IV salbutamol to halt labour and proceed to emergency percutaneous mitral balloon valvuloplasty
options.rationale: Beta-agonist tocolysis causes tachycardia and pulmonary oedema in severe MS and is contraindicated; valvuloplasty is considered antenatally in refractory disease, not in established labour at term (RHDAustralia; ESC 2018).

options.text: Proceed to immediate category 1 caesarean section under general anaesthesia for acute pulmonary oedema
options.rationale: Foetus is reassuring and labour is progressing; GA carries high haemodynamic risk in severe MS (induction-related tachycardia, swings in afterload). Vaginal birth with assisted second stage under regional anaesthesia is preferred if feasible (RANZCOG W-WH 22; ESC 2018).

options.text: Give an IV crystalloid bolus of 500 mL Hartmann's to maintain preload before considering further intervention
options.rationale: Fluid bolus in pulmonary congestion from severe MS worsens pulmonary oedema; preload must be preserved but not increased (RHDAustralia; ESC 2018).

options.text: Commence IV amiodarone infusion for presumed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
options.rationale: ECG shows sinus tachycardia, not AF; amiodarone is reserved for arrhythmia and carries foetal thyroid risk (RHDAustralia; AMH).

explanation.summary: Severe MS decompensates with tachycardia and Valsalva. Acute management is rate control with beta-blocker, diuresis, oxygen, and planned assisted second stage to deliver vaginally under regional analgesia (RHDAustralia; RANZCOG W-WH 22; ESC 2018 Pregnancy CVD).
key_points: Diastolic filling time is the critical variable in MS (ESC 2018)
Beta-blocker rate control is first-line acute therapy (RHDAustralia)
Avoid fluid bolus; manage pulmonary congestion with diuresis (ESC 2018)
Assisted second stage (vacuum/forceps) to limit Valsalva (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
GA caesarean carries higher haemodynamic risk than well-conducted epidural (ESC 2018)
Continue endocarditis-prophylaxis consideration per RHDAustralia for high-risk lesions
explanation.pearls: Anaesthetic plan should be written and reviewed by 32 weeks for any severe valvular lesion in pregnancy (RANZCOG W-WH 22).

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander intrapartum - rheumatic mitral stenosis decompensation

stem: A 24-year-old G2P0 woman in regional Victoria presents at 23+1 weeks by dating ultrasound requesting termination of pregnancy. Morphology ultrasound at 21 weeks showed a foetal cardiac anomaly initially read as a ventricular septal defect, but on tertiary review at 23 weeks was confirmed as hypoplastic left heart syndrome with associated coarctation. She has discussed the diagnosis with paediatric cardiology, including the staged Norwood pathway and its long-term survival and morbidity, and feels unable to proceed with the pregnancy. She has no medical comorbidities, her support network is intact, she has accessed counselling, and her first pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage. She specifically asks about the legal pathway in Victoria, whether she must travel interstate, and whether an ethics committee or panel needs to approve the procedure before she can be referred to the tertiary service She has no contraindication to mifepristone or prostaglandin induction.

data_table.Examination: SFH 24 cm; uterus consistent with dates
data_table.Investigations: Tertiary foetal echo: hypoplastic left heart syndrome with coarctation; karyotype normal; Hb 120 g/L

lead_in: Which statement best describes the lawful pathway for termination of pregnancy in Victoria at this gestation?

options.text: Termination is lawful on request without any additional consultation up to 24 weeks gestation in Victoria
options.rationale: Victorian law (Abortion Law Reform Act 2008) permits TOP on request only up to 24 weeks; this woman is 23+1 so meets the on-request limit, however the question asks about the lawful pathway most appropriate given the diagnosis - the standard practice at advanced gestation is to use the two-doctor pathway irrespective, but the lawful threshold remains 24 weeks for on-request. Note this option is technically true regarding the on-request limit; however the BEST description of the lawful pathway at this gestation, given foetal anomaly diagnosis and approaching the 24-week threshold, is the two-doctor pathway in option C.

options.text: Termination is unlawful after 20 weeks in Victoria and the patient must travel interstate to obtain care
options.rationale: Victoria has no 20-week prohibition; TOP is on-request to 24 weeks and lawful after 24 weeks if two doctors reasonably believe the procedure is appropriate (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

options.text: Termination is lawful: she is under the 24-week on-request limit and is referred to a tertiary service
options.rationale: Under the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008, TOP is lawful on request up to 24 weeks; after 24 weeks it requires two medical practitioners. At 23+1 weeks she is within the on-request limit. Standard practice for a confirmed major foetal anomaly at this gestation is referral to a tertiary service, which will offer feticide and induction of labour with multidisciplinary support (RANZCOG; Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

options.text: Termination requires approval by a formal hospital ethics committee at any gestation in Victoria
options.rationale: Victoria does not require ethics committee approval; the framework is the Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 with practitioner-led decision making (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

options.text: Termination is only lawful where the foetal anomaly is incompatible with life as defined by statute
options.rationale: There is no statutory list of qualifying anomalies; the test for TOP after 24 weeks is two-doctor reasonable belief that TOP is appropriate considering all relevant circumstances (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

explanation.summary: Victoria: TOP on-request to 24 weeks; after 24 weeks two doctors must reasonably believe it appropriate considering all relevant circumstances (Abortion Law Reform Act 2008). Late TOP for foetal anomaly is provided in tertiary services with feticide and IOL (RANZCOG; The Royal Women's).
key_points: Victoria: on-request to 24+0 (Abortion Law Reform Act 2008)
NSW: on-request to 22+0; after 22 weeks two specialists must agree (Abortion Law Reform Act 2019 NSW)
Qld: on-request to 22+0; after 22 weeks two doctors agree (Termination of Pregnancy Act 2018 Qld)
SA: on-request to 22+6; after 22+6 two doctors agree (Termination of Pregnancy Act 2021 SA)
WA: on-request to 23+0; after 23 weeks Ministerial panel of two specialists (Public Health Amendment Act 2023 WA)
Tasmania: on-request to 16+0; after 16 weeks two doctors agree (Reproductive Health Act 2013 Tas)
explanation.pearls: All states require conscientious objectors to refer to a non-objecting practitioner (RANZCOG TOP).

subtopic_detail: Termination of pregnancy - Australian state-specific gestational limits

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 presents at her general practice at 7+2 weeks by transvaginal ultrasound, having confirmed an unintended intrauterine pregnancy. She requests medical termination. She has no medical comorbidities, no allergies, BMI 23, takes no regular medications, has a confirmed intrauterine location with single live embryo CRL consistent with 7+2 weeks, has been screened and consented for medical TOP, and has discussed contraception. The GP is MS-2 Step certified. The patient lives 25 minutes from a hospital with 24-hour emergency services, has a supportive partner who can stay with her for 48 hours, and has clear arrangements for follow-up serum beta-hCG and a 2-week review. She understands the success rate, expected pain and bleeding, warning signs prompting ED return, and the need for confirmatory follow-up. She has read the MS-2 Step consumer information and asks the GP to confirm the exact dosing and interval she should expect.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen; cervix closed; small mobile uterus
data_table.Investigations: TVS: single live IUP, CRL 7+2 weeks; blood group O Rh positive; Hb 128 g/L
data_table.Counselling: TOP options discussed, contraception plan agreed (etonogestrel implant at follow-up)

lead_in: Which medical regimen is correct under the MS-2 Step Australian protocol for this gestation?

options.text: Mifepristone 200 mg oral, followed 36 to 48 hours later by misoprostol 800 microg buccal
options.rationale: The MS-2 Step Australian regimen for medical TOP up to 9+0 weeks is mifepristone 200 mg oral, followed 36-48 hours later by misoprostol 800 microg (buccal, sublingual or vaginal). Follow-up at 1-2 weeks with serum hCG or ultrasound (MS-2 Step Product Information; Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG TOP).

options.text: Mifepristone 600 mg oral, followed 24 hours later by misoprostol 400 microg oral
options.rationale: 600 mg mifepristone is the older regimen replaced because 200 mg is equally effective. 400 microg oral misoprostol has lower completion rates than 800 microg buccal/sublingual/vaginal (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).

options.text: Methotrexate 50 mg/m2 IM, followed 7 days later by misoprostol 800 microg vaginal
options.rationale: Methotrexate-misoprostol is used for medical management of ectopic pregnancy and historically for very early TOP, but is not the registered Australian protocol; MS-2 Step is the standard (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).

options.text: Misoprostol 800 microg vaginal alone, repeated every 3 hours up to 3 doses
options.rationale: Misoprostol-only regimens have lower success (~85%) than mifepristone-misoprostol (~95-98%) and are used only when mifepristone is unavailable (RANZCOG TOP; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Mifepristone 200 mg oral, followed immediately by misoprostol 800 microg buccal in the same visit
options.rationale: Simultaneous administration has lower efficacy than the 36-48 hour interval; the interval is required for mifepristone-induced cervical priming and progesterone receptor antagonism to take effect (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).

explanation.summary: MS-2 Step Australian medical TOP regimen: mifepristone 200 mg oral, then misoprostol 800 microg (buccal/sublingual/vaginal) 36-48 hours later, with follow-up at 1-2 weeks. Effective up to 9+0 weeks (MS-2 Step PI; Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG TOP).
key_points: Up to 9+0 weeks for MS-2 Step (Product Information)
Mifepristone 200 mg oral (not 600) (MS-2 Step PI)
Misoprostol 800 microg buccal/SL/PV at 36-48 h (MS-2 Step PI)
Follow-up at 1-2 weeks: serum hCG (>=80% drop) or USS (RANZCOG TOP)
Give anti-D 250 IU if Rh negative > 10 weeks per Australian schedule (Australian Red Cross)
Discuss contraception at the same visit (LARC same-day option) (Family Planning Australia)
explanation.pearls: MS-2 Step prescribers must be certified; pharmacy supply requires the certified dispenser network (MS Health).

subtopic_detail: Medical TOP - mifepristone-misoprostol regimen

stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 woman presents to ED 10 days after a medical TOP at 8 weeks using mifepristone-misoprostol from her GP. The procedure was reported as successful with passage of products on day 1 and tapering bleeding for 6 days. Over the last 36 hours she has had heavy bleeding soaking 1 large pad per hour, lower abdominal cramping, low-grade fever (37.8 deg C), foul-smelling discharge, and feels lightheaded on standing. She has no significant past history, takes no medications, blood group O Rh positive, and breastfed her two prior children. On arrival: BP 96/58, HR 116, T 37.9, RR 18, SpO2 99%. Abdomen tender suprapubically with guarding but no peritonism. Speculum: open external os with brisk bleeding and brown malodorous discharge, products visible at the os. Bedside USS: heterogeneous endometrial collection 38 mm with vascularity on colour Doppler and no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Bloods: Hb 88 g/L, WCC 16.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, lactate 1.6, group-and-hold sent. She has had no ectopic risk factors and the pre-TOP scan confirmed intrauterine pregnancy with normal adnexa.

data_table.Examination: Tender suprapubic with guarding; open cervical os with brisk bleed, products visible, foul discharge
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L, WCC 16.4, CRP 142, beta-hCG 1820 IU/L, lactate 1.6; USS endometrial collection 38 mm vascular

lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Outpatient repeat misoprostol 800 microg vaginal plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days, review in 48 hours
options.rationale: She has haemodynamic compromise, signs of sepsis and active bleeding; outpatient management is unsafe and inadequate source control (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Resuscitate with IV access, fluids, group-and-hold, and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, then proceed to urgent surgical evacuation of retained products
options.rationale: Septic incomplete abortion with haemorrhage requires resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole, or piperacillin-tazobactam per eTG), and source control by urgent surgical evacuation. Delaying evacuation prolongs the source of infection and bleeding (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Sepsis; RANZCOG PPH/Gynae emergencies).

options.text: Admit for IV oxytocin infusion with close observation, deferring evacuation pending blood culture results
options.rationale: Oxytocin has limited effect on a non-puerperal uterus, antibiotics should not wait for culture results in sepsis (within 1 hour per ANZICS), and source control must not be delayed (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV with observation and serial Hb without antibiotic cover until culture results
options.rationale: TXA is adjunctive but does not address source; delaying antibiotics in sepsis breaches the 1-hour standard and risks septic shock (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).

options.text: Intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 for presumed ongoing intrauterine pregnancy and discharge home to GP
options.rationale: Methotrexate addresses neither retained products nor sepsis, and is contraindicated in active infection (AMH; eTG).

explanation.summary: Septic incomplete abortion requires concurrent resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, and surgical source control by evacuation of retained products. This is a gynaecological emergency (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).
key_points: Antibiotics within 1 hour for sepsis (ANZICS Sepsis)
Source control: surgical evacuation of RPOC (RANZCOG TOP)
Antibiotic regimen: amp + gent + metronidazole or pip-taz (eTG Antibiotic)
Anti-D 250 IU if Rh negative and unsensitised (Australian Red Cross)
Major haemorrhage protocol if Hb continues to fall (RANZCOG PPH)
Inform original prescriber and arrange contraception/follow-up
explanation.pearls: Beware Clostridium sordellii / perfringens infection after medical TOP: profound shock without fever, leucocytosis and haemoconcentration (RANZCOG TOP).

subtopic_detail: Medical TOP - haemorrhage complication management

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 30 weeks gestation discloses intimate partner violence during a private antenatal visit after the midwife has seen her alone. Her partner has been verbally and physically abusive for 3 years; the frequency and severity have escalated over the past 6 weeks since she told him she was pregnant. Last week he choked her with both hands until she briefly lost consciousness, threatened to kill her if she left, and removed her mobile phone so she could not contact her sister. He has access to firearms because he is a licensed recreational shooter. She has two children aged 4 and 6 at home, no immediate family support locally, and no current Apprehended Domestic Violence Order. She has not previously disclosed to a clinician. She is asking what to do; she is frightened, oriented, and engaged. There is a social worker on site this afternoon and a state-funded specialist DV service with crisis accommodation available. The midwife has documented the disclosure in a separate confidential section of the record that the partner cannot access The midwife now needs to triage the risk level before activating crisis response.

data_table.Examination: Tender 4 cm bruise left flank, faint petechiae over neck, no current acute injury; SFH appropriate, FH 144
data_table.Social: Two children at home (4 and 6), no AVO, partner has firearms access, escalating frequency
data_table.Risk factors: Strangulation, threats to kill, controlling behaviour (phone removed), pregnancy, firearms access

lead_in: Which combination of features in this disclosure most strongly indicates EXTREME risk of intimate partner femicide and mandates immediate crisis-response activation?

options.text: Frequency of arguments and verbal abuse over the past 3 years without any physical injury
options.rationale: Verbal abuse and chronic frequency are markers of abuse but are not the discriminating extreme-risk features; the specific factors below are more strongly associated with lethality (1800RESPECT; ANROWS).

options.text: A past episode of pushing without injury approximately 18 months ago and now resolved
options.rationale: Historical low-severity violence is concerning but not in the same risk class as the cluster in option C (ANROWS).

options.text: Recent non-fatal strangulation, threat to kill, escalation in pregnancy, and firearm access
options.rationale: Non-fatal strangulation, an explicit threat to kill, escalation during pregnancy, abuser access to firearms, and coercive control (such as phone removal) are the strongest single predictors of intimate partner femicide. Together they meet criteria for EXTREME risk on the Danger Assessment and DVSAT and mandate immediate crisis-response activation (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; 1800RESPECT; ANROWS; Danger Assessment).

options.text: Disclosure occurring during the antenatal visit itself, without any other contextual features
options.rationale: The act of disclosure is positive engagement, not a risk marker; risk is determined by the content of the disclosure (ANROWS).

options.text: Absence of any Apprehended Domestic Violence Order despite the long abusive relationship history
options.rationale: Lack of AVO is common; it does not by itself stratify extreme risk (1800RESPECT).

explanation.summary: Non-fatal strangulation, explicit threats to kill, escalation in pregnancy, firearm access, and coercive control are the highest-yield extreme-risk features. Pregnancy is itself a peak-risk period. Activate crisis pathway, safety plan, and structured risk assessment (MARAM in Vic, DVSAT in NSW) (ANROWS; 1800RESPECT; MARAM; NSW DVSAT).
key_points: Non-fatal strangulation: ~7x increased risk of subsequent homicide (ANROWS)
Pregnancy is a peak risk period (1800RESPECT)
Firearm access is a major lethality factor (ANROWS)
Coercive control (phone removal, isolation) is a marker of severe pattern (1800RESPECT)
Use a structured framework: MARAM (Vic) or DVSAT (NSW)
Mandatory reporting of child exposure varies by state - check local Act
explanation.pearls: Strangulation may leave minimal external signs - ask specifically and examine for petechiae, voice change, dysphagia (ANROWS).

subtopic_detail: Domestic violence in pregnancy - escalation risk assessment

stem: A 34-year-old G4P3 woman in NSW discloses ongoing intimate partner violence during her postnatal home visit at day 14. There have been two physical assault episodes in the past 2 weeks, both witnessed by her 5- and 8-year-old children. The youngest child has begun bedwetting and the older child has been refusing school. The newborn is feeding well and gaining weight; there are no injuries on the infant. She is asking that nothing be reported to child protection because she fears the four children will be removed from her care. She has agreed to a safety plan, accepted a warm referral to a specialist DV service, and confirmed she is safe to remain at her sister's house tonight. The midwife is a mandated reporter under the NSW Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998, and exposure of children to intimate partner violence meets the threshold of Risk of Significant Harm under the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide.

data_table.Examination: Maternal: healing perineal repair, no acute injury, EPDS 16. Infant: alert, no injuries, weight gaining
data_table.Social: Two children witnessing assault; behavioural change in both; partner unaware of disclosure

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding child protection reporting?

options.text: Honour the woman's request not to report because reporting would breach therapeutic trust and engagement
options.rationale: Mandated reporters cannot suspend the legal duty at the patient's request; the threshold ('risk of significant harm') has been reached by witnessing repeated assault plus behavioural changes (NSW MRG; NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998).
source_refs: NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998

options.text: Report only the children's behavioural change to the GP and do not lodge any child protection report
options.rationale: Behavioural change in the context of witnessed intimate partner violence meets the NSW Risk of Significant Harm threshold and triggers mandated reporting (NSW MRG).

options.text: Lodge a Risk of Significant Harm report to the NSW Child Protection Helpline and continue the safety plan
options.rationale: Exposure of children to intimate partner violence meets the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide threshold for Risk of Significant Harm. Reporting does not equal removal; the report triggers a structured response that can include in-home support. The midwife should explain her legal duty, continue the safety plan and warm DV referrals, and document the woman's agreement to ongoing engagement (NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998; NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide; NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

options.text: Contact NSW Police directly to seek an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order on behalf of the woman
options.rationale: ADVO applications are typically made by police or the protected person; clinicians can encourage and support but the immediate mandated step is child protection reporting because children are involved (NSW MRG; NSW Police ADVO guidance).

options.text: Wait until the next clinic visit in a fortnight to allow the woman more time to consider
options.rationale: Delaying a RoSH report once the threshold is met breaches the mandated reporter duty and risks ongoing harm to the children (NSW MRG).

explanation.summary: In NSW, witnessing repeated domestic violence with behavioural change in the children meets the Risk of Significant Harm threshold and mandates a report to the NSW Child Protection Helpline, alongside continued safety planning and specialist DV referral. Transparency with the mother is best practice (NSW MRG; NSW Health DV Routine Screening).
key_points: Risk of Significant Harm threshold triggers mandatory reporting (NSW MRG)
All states have mandatory reporter pathways; thresholds and scope differ (each state Act)
Witnessing IPV counts as exposure to harm (NSW MRG)
Report does not replace DV safety planning - both proceed (NSW Health)
Tell the parent (when safe) that a report is being made
explanation.pearls: Use the NSW MRG decision tree on the spot if uncertain about threshold (NSW MRG).

sources.label: NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998

subtopic_detail: Domestic violence - mandatory reporting of child exposure

stem: A 26-year-old woman attends her general practice for review. She disclosed intimate partner violence at her last consultation 4 weeks ago, when the GP drew up a written safety plan with code words, a packed bag at her sister's house, and the 1800RESPECT number. Today she reports that since the last argument 3 weeks ago her partner has been kind and attentive, has brought her flowers, agreed to attend a couples counsellor, and promised that the violence will not happen again. She is reconsidering whether she still needs the safety plan. She has no children, owns the rental jointly with her partner, has independent income, and her parents live in another state. She is calm, oriented, and has no acute injuries. She specifically asks whether the change in her partner's behaviour means the cycle is over and whether joint couples counselling would now be safe.

data_table.Examination: No acute injury; old healed bruising right upper arm
data_table.Social: No children; joint rental; independent income; family interstate; partner currently in 'reconciliation' phase per her description

lead_in: Which is the most accurate framing of the current situation to share with this patient?

options.text: Reassure her that the apology phase usually means violence will not recur and the safety plan can be discarded
options.rationale: This misrepresents the cycle and risks re-victimisation. Recurrence after a reconciliation/honeymoon phase is the typical pattern (1800RESPECT; ANROWS).

options.text: The reconciliation phase is part of the cycle of abuse; tension and incident phases typically recur
options.rationale: Walker's cycle of abuse describes a tension-building phase, an acute incident, and a reconciliation or honeymoon phase. The reconciliation phase usually relapses without specialist perpetrator intervention; the safety plan therefore remains important. Couples counselling is contraindicated in active intimate partner violence because of escalation risk after sessions (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; 1800RESPECT; RACGP White Book).

options.text: Insist she leave the relationship today as the only safe and effective option available
options.rationale: Leaving is often the highest-risk period; safety planning, autonomy preservation and supportive accompaniment are the correct approach, not coercive directives (ANROWS; 1800RESPECT).

options.text: Recommend joint couples counselling because the partner has now agreed to attend the session together
options.rationale: Couples therapy is contraindicated in active intimate partner violence due to disclosure-retaliation risk (1800RESPECT; ANROWS).

options.text: Discharge her from clinical follow-up because she now feels safe and is competent to decide for herself
options.rationale: Autonomy is respected, but offering continued review and access to support is standard - the cycle pattern means new opportunities for engagement should remain open (1800RESPECT).

explanation.summary: Cycle of abuse (tension, incident, reconciliation) means that the apology/honeymoon phase commonly precedes recurrence. Safety plans should be preserved and updated; couples counselling is contraindicated during active IPV; leaving is itself a high-risk transition requiring planning (1800RESPECT; ANROWS).
key_points: Walker cycle: tension -> incident -> reconciliation -> repeat (1800RESPECT)
Leaving is the highest-risk period (ANROWS)
Couples therapy contraindicated in active IPV (1800RESPECT)
Safety plans: code word, packed bag, important documents, escape route (1800RESPECT)
Document outside hand-held notes; offer 1800RESPECT contact
explanation.pearls: Most women leave 5-7 times before final departure; do not measure success by single-visit resolution (ANROWS).

subtopic_detail: Domestic violence - cycle and safety planning

stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to ED 5 days (120 hours) after non-consensual unprotected vaginal intercourse with an unknown male at a party. She did not present sooner because of fear of judgement. She is otherwise well, has no medical history, no allergies, takes no regular medications, has a regular 28-day menstrual cycle with last period 11 days ago (so currently around day 11 and within the fertile window), and has previously had her three-dose HPV vaccine and a full primary hepatitis B course as a child. She has had no further intercourse since. She is uncertain about reporting to police but wants medical care. The local sexual assault medical service is contactable, the on-call gynaecology registrar can insert a copper IUD today, and the assailant's HIV status is unknown. She is asking what can still be offered for emergency contraception and infection prevention given the 5-day window since exposure, and she is open to all options including a long-acting method She declines police involvement today and asks specifically about emergency contraception that will still work at the 120-hour mark and about whether STI screening can be done at the same visit.

data_table.Examination: No acute injury; healing 1 cm abrasion left knee; speculum: no acute trauma, no visible discharge
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative; baseline HIV Ag/Ab, HBsAg/HBsAb/anti-HBc, HCV Ab, syphilis EIA, NAATs for chlamydia/gonorrhoea/trichomonas sent
data_table.Cycle: Day 11 of regular 28-day cycle; previous LMP 11 days ago

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate component of management at this time?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg orally as a single-dose emergency contraceptive within the 72-hour licensed window
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel EC efficacy is limited to within 96 hours of unprotected intercourse and is not first-line beyond that window (Family Planning Australia; eTG).

options.text: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg orally as a single-dose emergency contraceptive within the 120-hour licensed window
options.rationale: Ulipristal extends the window to 120 hours but evidence is for up to 120 hours post-intercourse, and at exactly 120 hours efficacy is borderline; the copper IUD is the more reliable option still available now (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Copper IUD insertion as emergency contraception, with empirical STI prophylaxis and sexual assault referral
options.rationale: Copper IUD inserted up to 5 days after the earliest predicted ovulation is the most effective emergency contraception (failure rate <1%) and is the only option licensed beyond 120 hours. At day 11 of a 28-day cycle she remains within the fertile window. Empirical STI prophylaxis (ceftriaxone, azithromycin or doxycycline, metronidazole) and warm referral to the sexual assault service complete management (RANZCOG; ASHM Post-Exposure Prophylaxis Guidelines).

options.text: Commence HIV non-occupational post-exposure prophylaxis with tenofovir-emtricitabine-dolutegravir for a full 28-day course
options.rationale: HIV PEP must be commenced within 72 hours of exposure; at 120 hours the window has closed and PEP is not indicated (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

options.text: Accelerated hepatitis B vaccination course because the patient is presumed unprotected after childhood priming
options.rationale: She has a documented completed primary hepatitis B course; check anti-HBs titre rather than re-vaccinate (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

explanation.summary: At 120 hours post-assault, the most effective EC option still available is the copper IUD (up to 5 days). Oral EC efficacy is borderline; HIV PEP is no longer indicated. Empirical STI prophylaxis and sexual assault service referral remain appropriate (Family Planning Australia; Australasian STI Management Guidelines; ASHM; RANZCOG).
key_points: Levonorgestrel EC: up to 96 hours (Family Planning Australia)
Ulipristal EC: up to 120 hours (Family Planning Australia)
Copper IUD EC: up to 5 days after earliest ovulation or 5 days after intercourse (Family Planning Australia)
HIV PEP: must start within 72 hours (ASHM)
Forensic kit: typically <72 h, some samples up to 7 days (RANZCOG)
Sexual assault psychological support remains available regardless of timing
explanation.pearls: Copper IUD is the only EC method that simultaneously provides ongoing contraception (Family Planning Australia).

subtopic_detail: Sexual assault - presentation beyond 72 hours

stem: A 19-year-old woman presents to ED 18 hours after a night out, with no memory of several hours of the evening, having woken in an unfamiliar bed beside someone she did not know, with disarranged clothing. She is concerned that her drink was spiked. She last urinated on waking 1 hour ago and has had three sips of water since. She has had no alcohol or illicit drugs to her knowledge other than two drinks she ordered and saw poured. She has no known medical conditions and takes no regular medications. She is alert, oriented, distressed but cooperative. The local sexual assault medical service is contactable and a forensic medical examiner is available within the hour. She has not decided whether to report to police. The forensic examiner will offer toxicology and asks which sample to collect first given the long window since exposure and the short half-lives of common drug-facilitated sexual assault agents such as GHB and benzodiazepines.

data_table.Examination: No acute injury identified; speculum to be performed by forensic examiner if she consents
data_table.Investigations: Pending forensic samples, baseline STI screen, beta-hCG
data_table.Timing: 18 hours since suspected event; first void available; minimal hydration

lead_in: Which sample provides the highest yield for detection of common drug-facilitated sexual assault agents at this presentation?

options.text: A clotted serum sample taken now for benzodiazepine and GHB quantification analysis
options.rationale: Many DFSA agents (notably GHB) have short serum half-lives (~30-60 minutes); by 18 hours serum is typically negative even when urine remains positive (RANZCOG sexual assault; Forensic and Applied Toxicology Australia).

options.text: First-catch urine sample now and a repeat urine sample at the next void
options.rationale: Urine is the highest-yield matrix for drug-facilitated sexual assault toxicology because most agents (GHB, benzodiazepines, ketamine) have short serum half-lives but concentrate in urine and can be detected for 12 to 96 hours depending on the agent. A first-catch sample now plus a repeat at the next void maximises detection (RANZCOG; NSW Health Sexual Assault Services; ANZFSS forensic toxicology guidance).

options.text: Scalp hair sample taken now for later segmental analysis after a 4-week growth window
options.rationale: Hair toxicology has a role but the optimal timing is typically 3-5 weeks after exposure to allow the dosed segment to grow above the scalp; not the highest-yield sample at 18 hours (RANZCOG sexual assault).

options.text: Gastric aspirate via nasogastric tube to recover any residual ingested drug from the stomach
options.rationale: NG aspirate is not standard in DFSA workup and is invasive without yield (RANZCOG sexual assault).

options.text: Oral mucosal swab to recover any drug residues from saliva and the buccal mucosa surface
options.rationale: Oral fluid has a shorter detection window than urine and is not the preferred forensic toxicology matrix for DFSA at 18 hours (RANZCOG sexual assault).

explanation.summary: Urine is the highest-yield forensic toxicology matrix in suspected drug-facilitated sexual assault within the first 24 hours; collect first void plus serial samples. Hair analysis is reserved for late presentations 3-5 weeks later (RANZCOG sexual assault; Family Planning Australia).
key_points: Urine: best matrix to ~24 h (RANZCOG)
Serum: short window (~12 h for benzo, <6 h for GHB) (RANZCOG)
Hair: collect at 3-5 weeks (RANZCOG)
Always handle via chain of custody (RANZCOG)
Concurrent EC, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP assessment, support
explanation.pearls: Encourage the patient to not pass urine without forensic collection if she presents within 12-24 h of suspected event (RANZCOG sexual assault).

subtopic_detail: Sexual assault - drug-facilitated and toxicology evidence

stem: A 29-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed at 5 weeks postpartum at her general practice. She has focal epilepsy controlled on carbamazepine 400 mg BD (level stable, no seizures for 3 years), is exclusively breastfeeding her baby (infant weight tracking the 50th centile, no concerns at the maternal-child health nurse review), and had a normal vaginal birth with no thromboembolic history, no migraine, normotensive, BMI 24, non-smoker, and a normal Hb of 122 g/L. She has read about long-acting reversible contraception and is aware that carbamazepine is an enzyme inducer. She wishes to space the next pregnancy by at least 3 years and prefers a method that does not require daily adherence or rely on her remembering to attend for 3-monthly injections. She is happy with all options being on the table, including LARCs and combined methods, and asks the GP to recommend the most reliable option that will not be undermined by her antiepileptic. Her neurologist is aware and supportive of any contraceptive choice She has no history of menorrhagia and no copper allergy, and lactational amenorrhoea alone is not acceptable to her given the 3-year spacing she wants.

data_table.Medications: Carbamazepine 400 mg BD; folic acid 5 mg daily (planned for future pregnancy)
data_table.Pregnancy plans: Spacing at least 3 years; LARC preferred

lead_in: Which contraceptive option is the most appropriate first-line recommendation?

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
options.rationale: Enzyme-inducing AEDs (including carbamazepine) significantly reduce etonogestrel plasma levels via CYP3A4 induction; the implant is NOT recommended (UKMEC 3) in women on enzyme inducers because contraceptive efficacy is reduced (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyloestradiol 30 microg with levonorgestrel) taken daily
options.rationale: Enzyme inducers reduce COCP efficacy and the COCP is generally avoided <6 weeks postpartum if breastfeeding due to milk supply and VTE concerns; not first-line here (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (norethisterone 350 microg) taken at the same time every day
options.rationale: POP efficacy is reduced by enzyme inducers; not recommended (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine device (Mirena) or copper IUD inserted as a LARC
options.rationale: Intrauterine devices (LNG-IUD and copper IUD) deliver hormone locally or are non-hormonal and have efficacy that is NOT reduced by enzyme inducers; both are UKMEC 1 in women on enzyme-inducing AEDs and compatible with breastfeeding. They provide the long-acting reversible spacing this woman requests (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Drug Interactions with Hormonal Contraception).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg intramuscularly every 12 weeks as her contraceptive method
options.rationale: DMPA contraceptive efficacy is not significantly reduced by enzyme inducers (UKMEC 1), so it is acceptable; however it is not the SINGLE BEST recommendation when an IUD is suitable and the woman wants long-term spacing without quarterly injections, and DMPA has bone density and weight effects to discuss (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

explanation.summary: Enzyme-inducing AEDs reduce efficacy of oestrogen, progestogen-only pill and the etonogestrel implant. IUDs (LNG and copper) and DMPA are not significantly affected. IUD is preferred as long-acting, non-daily, breastfeeding-compatible (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Drug Interactions).
key_points: Enzyme inducers: carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarb, primidone, topiramate >200 mg, rifampicin (FSRH)
Affected: COCP, POP, implant, transdermal patch, ring
Not affected: LNG-IUD, copper IUD, DMPA (FSRH)
If on enzyme inducer and COCP required: 50 microg EE or alternative method (FSRH)
Lamotrigine, levetiracetam, sodium valproate are NOT enzyme inducers
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine levels are themselves reduced by COCP - bidirectional interaction; this is opposite to the carbamazepine direction (FSRH Drug Interactions).

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - breastfeeding + enzyme-inducing AED

stem: A 33-year-old G1P1 is reviewed at 6 weeks postpartum following a left lower-limb deep vein thrombosis diagnosed 2 weeks ago and now on therapeutic warfarin (target INR 2 to 3) after initial enoxaparin. She is bottle feeding by choice (lactation suppressed), normotensive, BMI 28, non-smoker, with no history of migraine and no other medical history. She asks for the most reliable contraception she can use. She has decided she does not want further children at this stage but is not ready to consider permanent sterilisation. She is happy with all reversible options, including LARCs, and asks the GP to confirm which methods are unsafe given the recent DVT, particularly the combined hormonal options that her sister uses. She has read about UKMEC categories online and wants to understand which method she should avoid She is on warfarin for at least 3 more months and may extend to 6 months.

data_table.Examination: Healing left calf, no swelling; uterus involuted; non-lactating

lead_in: Which contraceptive option is contraindicated (UKMEC category 4) in this woman?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine device (Mirena) inserted at this 6-week review
options.rationale: LNG-IUD is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE; not contraindicated. Insertion in an anticoagulated patient requires care for procedural bleeding but is acceptable (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
options.rationale: Etonogestrel implant is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE; usable. Note local bruising risk on insertion (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (norethisterone 350 microg) taken daily at the same time
options.rationale: POP is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Combined hormonal contraception (COCP, vaginal ring, or transdermal patch) at any dose
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception (oestrogen-containing) is UKMEC 4 (unacceptable) in current/past VTE because the prothrombotic effect of synthetic oestrogen further increases recurrence risk; anticoagulation does not negate the contraindication (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia; RCOG VTE in pregnancy guidance).

options.text: Copper intrauterine device inserted as a non-hormonal LARC at this review
options.rationale: Copper IUD is UKMEC 1 in current/past VTE; it is non-hormonal and does not affect thrombotic risk (FSRH UKMEC).

explanation.summary: Combined hormonal contraception is UKMEC 4 in current or past VTE because oestrogen increases recurrence risk; concurrent anticoagulation does not change this. Progestogen-only methods and IUDs are acceptable (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).
key_points: Current/past VTE: CHC UKMEC 4 (FSRH)
Acceptable: copper IUD (UKMEC 1), LNG-IUD/POP/implant/DMPA (UKMEC 2) (FSRH)
Insert IUD with INR confirmed in range and consider haemostatic precautions (FSRH)
Warfarin and DOACs do not interact with hormonal contraception efficacy (FSRH)
Counsel re return of fertility - prompt with most methods on cessation
explanation.pearls: First-degree family history of unprovoked VTE plus known thrombophilia raises CHC to UKMEC 4 even without personal history (FSRH UKMEC).

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - post-VTE on anticoagulation

stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed 6 weeks postpartum at her general practice. She had a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass 3 years ago with weight loss from 142 kg to 78 kg (current BMI 28, stable). She is bottle feeding by choice, normotensive, non-smoker, no migraine, no VTE history, and is on regular vitamin and iron supplementation post-bariatric. She is asking the GP for contraception and is happy with all options, including LARCs. Her last pregnancy was carefully monitored post-bariatric and was uncomplicated other than mild iron deficiency. She specifically asks whether she can simply take her sister's combined oral contraceptive pill, because she has heard that any oral hormonal method is reliable. She also asks whether a return of regular cycles is required before she starts a method, given she is not yet menstruating because she is using progesterone-containing analgesia Her dietician has confirmed she is taking her bariatric multivitamin daily and her ferritin has normalised, and the GP is happy to insert a LARC at this visit if appropriate She specifically asks whether oral pills will still work post-bypass.

data_table.Examination: Well, lactation suppressed; surgical scars from RYGB
data_table.Medications: Multivitamin, iron, vitamin B12 IM 3-monthly, calcium + vitamin D

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate consideration when selecting contraception for this woman?

options.text: Oral hormonal contraception is unaffected because Roux-en-Y bypass does not alter small-molecule absorption
options.rationale: Roux-en-Y bypass alters oral hormonal absorption and reduces COCP and POP efficacy; FSRH advises against reliance on oral methods after malabsorptive bariatric procedures (FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Overweight/Obesity and Contraception).

options.text: Long-acting reversible methods are preferred because oral hormonal absorption is reduced post-bypass
options.rationale: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass bypasses the proximal small bowel and reduces absorption of oral hormonal contraceptives, which lowers their reliability. LARCs (LNG-IUD, copper IUD, etonogestrel implant, DMPA) do not depend on enteric absorption and are first-line after bariatric bypass surgery (Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare; UKMEC; RANZCOG).

options.text: Combined hormonal contraception is preferred to ensure cycle regularity post-bariatric surgery in this patient
options.rationale: Oral COCP efficacy is reduced after RYGB; transdermal/vaginal CHC may also be considered but post-bariatric weight loss instability and weight rebound risk make LARC the preferred first-line, not CHC for cycle control (FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

options.text: Await return of regular menstrual cycles before starting any contraception in this patient
options.rationale: Fertility returns rapidly postpartum (and dramatically improves post-bariatric); contraception should be in place to avoid early conception (Family Planning Australia; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

options.text: Permanent sterilisation should be recommended because pregnancy is unsafe after bariatric surgery
options.rationale: Pregnancy after bariatric is safe with planned spacing (12-24 months recommended) and adequate nutritional surveillance; recommending sterilisation is not appropriate without patient-led discussion (RANZCOG; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

explanation.summary: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and other malabsorptive bariatric procedures reduce oral hormonal contraceptive absorption. LARCs (LNG-IUD, copper IUD, implant) and DMPA are the preferred choices because they bypass intestinal absorption. Restrictive procedures alone do not have the same effect (FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Overweight/Obesity; Family Planning Australia).
key_points: Malabsorptive procedures: Roux-en-Y, BPD, duodenal switch (FSRH)
Restrictive only: sleeve gastrectomy, gastric band - oral methods acceptable (FSRH)
LARC bypasses absorption issue (FSRH)
Plan pregnancy 12-24 months post-bariatric (RANZCOG)
Folate, B12, iron, calcium, vitamin D surveillance pre-pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Post-bariatric the patient may be hyperfertile compared to her pre-operative state; do not assume the previous infertility persists (FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - post-bariatric surgery

stem: A 30-year-old G1P1 woman with well-controlled HIV (CD4 720, viral load <50 copies/mL on tenofovir alafenamide-emtricitabine-bictegravir) is reviewed at 6 weeks postpartum. She is formula feeding per current Australian guidance, an option chosen after counselling once viral suppression was sustained, and the infant has completed post-exposure prophylaxis per ASHM. She is normotensive, BMI 25, non-smoker, no migraine, no VTE history, and no other comorbidity. She wants reliable contraception for at least 2 years, preferably long-acting and not requiring daily adherence. Her HIV physician confirms she will remain on bictegravir-based therapy and is not on any enzyme-inducing antiretroviral. She specifically asks whether the implant will still work on her HIV regimen and whether she needs efavirenz to be added for the implant to be effective Her infectious-diseases physician has cleared any contraceptive method Her HIV physician noted no enzyme-inducing antiretrovirals on her regimen.

data_table.Medications: Tenofovir alafenamide-emtricitabine-bictegravir daily; no other medication

lead_in: Which contraceptive option is most appropriate without significant interaction concerns?

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
options.rationale: Bictegravir/integrase inhibitor-based regimens (including tenofovir alafenamide-emtricitabine-bictegravir) do not significantly affect hormonal contraceptive efficacy; the etonogestrel implant is suitable. NNRTI-based regimens (efavirenz) and protease inhibitor/ritonavir-boosted regimens can reduce hormonal contraceptive levels (ASHM HIV Management; FSRH Drug Interactions; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill in the immediate postpartum period because she is not breastfeeding
options.rationale: CHC is contraindicated <6 weeks postpartum due to VTE risk regardless of breastfeeding (UKMEC 4 <3 weeks postpartum, UKMEC 3 from 3-6 weeks); efficacy with bictegravir is preserved but the postpartum VTE concern dominates (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal implant only if efavirenz is added to her antiretroviral regimen
options.rationale: Efavirenz (an NNRTI) is an enzyme inducer that REDUCES etonogestrel efficacy; adding it would worsen interactions (FSRH Drug Interactions; ASHM).

options.text: Avoid all hormonal methods because of HIV-related immune effects on contraceptive efficacy
options.rationale: Well-controlled HIV does not alter hormonal contraceptive efficacy; the relevant issue is specific drug interactions, not the underlying HIV (FSRH UKMEC; ASHM).

options.text: Permanent surgical sterilisation as the only safe contraceptive option in women with HIV
options.rationale: There is no contraceptive method that is unsafe purely on the basis of well-controlled HIV; reproductive autonomy and full method choice apply (ASHM HIV Management; UKMEC).

explanation.summary: Modern integrase inhibitor regimens (bictegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir) do not significantly interact with hormonal contraception; the etonogestrel implant and other LARCs are appropriate. NNRTIs (efavirenz, nevirapine) and ritonavir-boosted protease inhibitors reduce contraceptive efficacy. CHC has the usual <6-week postpartum VTE restrictions (ASHM HIV Management; FSRH Drug Interactions; FSRH UKMEC).
key_points: Integrase inhibitors (bictegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir): no major interaction (FSRH)
NNRTIs (efavirenz, nevirapine): reduce hormonal contraception (FSRH)
Ritonavir-boosted PIs: reduce hormonal contraception (FSRH)
IUDs (LNG and copper) are UKMEC 1 in HIV with no AIDS-defining illness (FSRH UKMEC)
Australian infant feeding guidance: formula feeding currently advised even if maternal U=U (ASHM)
explanation.pearls: Always check the specific ART regimen before assuming interactions; integrase-only era has changed the rules (FSRH Drug Interactions).

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - HIV on ART

subtopic: Indigenous health (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander)
tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health
stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 Aboriginal woman from a regional town presents at 11 weeks for booking with her local ACCHO. Her two previous pregnancies were complicated by gestational diabetes managed with diet and metformin, and both babies were macrosomic (birth weights 4.3 kg and 4.5 kg with shoulder dystocia in the second). Between pregnancies she lost touch with primary care and has not had her glucose checked. Her BMI is 32 and BP is 122/76. There is a strong family history of type 2 diabetes (mother, two siblings). At booking, the GP arranges fasting venous plasma glucose and HbA1c as part of the early antenatal panel recommended by ADIPS and RANZCOG for high-risk women. Results: fasting venous plasma glucose 6.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.7% (50 mmol/mol), urine ketones negative. She is otherwise well, on folic acid and iodine, and asks whether she has GDM or something different.

data_table.Investigations: Fasting plasma glucose 6.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.7% (50 mmol/mol), urine ketones negative
data_table.Past obstetric: Two prior GDM, macrosomic infants 4.3 and 4.5 kg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation and next step?

options.text: Reassure that values are normal in pregnancy and re-screen with 75 g OGTT at 24-28 weeks
options.rationale: Fasting plasma glucose >=6.1 and/or HbA1c >=6.5% (48 mmol/mol) in early pregnancy meets ADIPS criteria for diabetes in pregnancy (likely pre-existing T2DM), not GDM; treating as normal misses pre-existing disease (ADIPS Hyperglycaemia in Pregnancy 2022).

options.text: Diagnose diabetes in pregnancy, commence lifestyle measures and metformin, refer to multidisciplinary clinic
options.rationale: FPG >=7.0 or HbA1c >=6.5% at any time in pregnancy meets criteria for diabetes in pregnancy (likely pre-existing type 2), not GDM. Management mirrors pre-existing T2DM in pregnancy: lifestyle intervention, metformin, early multidisciplinary diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic review, a detailed foetal anomaly scan, and early ophthalmology and renal screening for undiagnosed end-organ damage (ADIPS; RANZCOG; NACCHO).

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes on the FPG threshold of >=5.1 and commence dietary advice only
options.rationale: FPG 5.1-6.0 with no diabetes-range result is GDM; this woman exceeds the diabetes threshold and HbA1c is also in the diabetes range. Treating as GDM under-recognises the diagnosis and undertreats (ADIPS).

options.text: Repeat the fasting plasma glucose in 4 weeks before initiating any pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Delaying intervention by 4 weeks misses critical early-pregnancy organogenesis-period glycaemic control and risks higher congenital anomaly rates (ADIPS; RANZCOG Diabetes in Pregnancy).

options.text: Commence insulin glargine immediately as monotherapy without any metformin or lifestyle intervention being trialled
options.rationale: First-line pharmacotherapy in pregnancy for overt T2DM is usually metformin with stepwise insulin addition; starting insulin alone without lifestyle and metformin is not the standard initial approach (ADIPS; RANZCOG; AMH).

explanation.summary: Early-pregnancy FPG >=7.0 mmol/L OR HbA1c >=6.5% OR random >=11.1 = overt diabetes in pregnancy (typically previously undiagnosed T2DM). Management = treat as pre-existing diabetes with MDT clinic, tight targets, early anomaly screening, retinal and renal baseline. Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women have higher T2DM prevalence and benefit from integrated culturally safe care (ADIPS Hyperglycaemia in Pregnancy; RANZCOG Diabetes in Pregnancy; RACGP).
key_points: FPG >=5.1, 1h >=10.0, 2h >=8.5 mmol/L on 75 g OGTT = GDM (ADIPS)
FPG >=7.0 or HbA1c >=6.5% (48 mmol/mol) or random >=11.1 = overt diabetes in pregnancy (ADIPS)
Treat overt diabetes in pregnancy as pre-existing T2DM (RANZCOG)
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander-specific early screening recommended at booking (ADIPS; RACGP)
Metformin + insulin if needed; glargine and aspart commonly used (AMH)
explanation.pearls: Macrosomia + prior GDM + family history of T2DM mandates early-booking FPG/HbA1c, not waiting for 24-28 weeks (ADIPS).

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health - hyperglycaemia in pregnancy

stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 woman in Perth, Western Australia presents at 11 weeks gestation by ultrasound requesting termination of pregnancy. She has no medical comorbidities, takes no medication, and has discussed contraception. She has had options counselling and is certain of her decision. There is no foetal anomaly, no maternal medical indication, and her decision is based on personal and social circumstances. She specifically asks whether her partner must consent, whether a mandatory waiting period applies between first consultation and procedure, and whether she can have medical TOP or must have a surgical TOP, because friends have told her conflicting accounts of the law in WA. Her GP is one of the medical practitioners credentialled to provide TOP under the new Act.

data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound singleton live IUP 11+0 weeks; blood group O Rh positive

lead_in: Under the current WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023, which statement is correct regarding this woman's TOP?

options.text: Unlawful in WA under any circumstances; the patient must travel interstate to access termination
options.rationale: TOP is lawful in WA under the Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023, which decriminalised abortion and aligned WA with other states (WA Public Health Amendment Act 2023).

options.text: Lawful on request up to 23 weeks with one practitioner; after 23 weeks needs two specialists
options.rationale: Under the WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023, TOP is available on the woman's request up to 23 weeks with one medical practitioner. After 23 weeks two specialist medical practitioners must consider it appropriate. There is no partner consent requirement and no mandatory waiting period; medical and surgical TOP are both available (Government of WA Health; RANZCOG).

options.text: Requires the consent of the patient's partner before the procedure can be performed in WA
options.rationale: No Australian jurisdiction requires partner consent for TOP (WA Public Health Amendment Act 2023; RANZCOG TOP).

options.text: Requires a 7-day mandatory waiting period from the first consultation before the procedure in WA
options.rationale: Mandatory waiting periods are not part of the WA Act; some jurisdictions internationally impose them but WA does not (WA Public Health Amendment Act 2023).

options.text: Must be performed surgically at 11 weeks because medical termination is not available in WA
options.rationale: Medical TOP using MS-2 Step is available throughout Australia including WA, where the prescriber is appropriately certified (MS-2 Step PI; WA Department of Health).

explanation.summary: The WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023 decriminalised TOP and allows on-request access up to 23 weeks with one medical practitioner. After 23 weeks a Ministerial-approved panel of two specialists must agree. Other jurisdictional comparators are in the key points (WA Public Health Amendment Act 2023; RANZCOG TOP).
key_points: WA: on-request to 23 weeks; >23 weeks panel of two specialists (WA Act 2023)
Vic: 24 weeks on-request (Vic Act 2008)
NSW: 22 weeks on-request (NSW Act 2019)
Qld: 22 weeks on-request (Qld Act 2018)
SA: 22+6 on-request (SA Act 2021)
Tas: 16 weeks on-request (Tas Act 2013)
No state requires partner consent
Conscientious objectors must refer (RANZCOG TOP)
explanation.pearls: Cross-jurisdictional travel still occurs for late TOP due to capacity rather than legality (RANZCOG TOP).

subtopic_detail: TOP - state-specific gestational law - WA

stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 woman is 34 weeks pregnant and has an active Apprehended Domestic Violence Order in NSW against her former partner, who is the father of the unborn child. The order prohibits him contacting her or approaching within 100 metres of her residence and workplace. She presents to the maternity day-assessment unit for routine review and is anxious because her former partner has been ringing the hospital switchboard asking for her appointment times and 'information about his child'. She is fully engaged in antenatal care, has a written safety plan, is supported by a hospital social worker, and has a planned hospital birth. She asks specifically whether the ADVO applies inside the hospital, whether her former partner has any right to be told her appointment times because he is the father, and what the hospital can do to ensure he cannot find out when she will be in the building.

data_table.Social: Active ADVO; ex-partner attempting information access via switchboard; social worker engaged

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next action with respect to her hospital information access and safety?

options.text: Provide the former partner with appointment times because he has a paternal right to information about the unborn child
options.rationale: There is no automatic paternal right to maternal health information; the woman is the patient and consent governs disclosure. Disclosure would breach NSW Health privacy and the ADVO protective intent (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; NSW Health Privacy Manual).

options.text: Flag the medical record as confidential, instruct switchboard and reception not to release any information, and notify security
options.rationale: Hospital information access is a critical component of the safety plan in active intimate partner violence with an ADVO. The medical record should be flagged as confidential, the ADVO documented, switchboard and reception instructed not to release any information, the social worker and hospital security notified, and the safety plan for arrival and discharge confirmed. Paternity does not override the ADVO or confer a right to maternal appointment information (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; NSW Crimes (Domestic and Personal Violence) Act 2007).

options.text: Transfer her care to a different hospital to break the trail without involving her in the decision
options.rationale: Transfer without patient agreement breaks continuity, undermines the relationship she has built with the team, and is not the recommended approach (NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

options.text: Tell the partner the ADVO does not apply to the hospital so he may attend appointments
options.rationale: ADVOs apply across all locations as worded by the magistrate; hospital is not exempt. Inviting attendance contradicts the order (NSW Police ADVO guidance; NSW DV Routine Screening).

options.text: Restrict her from public maternity care until the ADVO is formally upgraded by the court
options.rationale: Restricting care is unsafe and inappropriate; the response is to make care safe, not to withdraw it (NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

explanation.summary: When an active ADVO/DVO is in place, hospital response is to restrict-flag the record, brief switchboard and reception, brief security, coordinate with the social worker, and plan safe arrival and discharge for the birth. There is no automatic paternal right to maternal health information (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; NSW Health Privacy Manual).
key_points: Patient consent governs disclosure of her health information (NSW Health Privacy)
ADVO applies across all locations including hospital (NSW Police)
Restricted record flag is a routine NSW Health mechanism
Coordinate with hospital security and social work for arrival/discharge (NSW Health)
Maintain access to care, not withdraw it
explanation.pearls: Document the ADVO conditions in the safety plan section of the record; review at each contact (NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

subtopic_detail: DV - DVO/AVO interaction with antenatal care planning

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman undergoes elective surgical TOP at 13 weeks by dilation and evacuation in a day-procedure unit. The procedure itself is uneventful but in recovery 30 minutes later she is found to be soaking the pad with bright red blood. She has no significant past medical history, is on no anticoagulation, has no known bleeding diathesis, BMI 26, and no prior caesarean section. On urgent review: BP 88/52, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 99%, T 36.7, alert but pale and clammy. Abdominal palpation reveals a soft, poorly contracted, palpable uterus midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis. Speculum: brisk red bleeding from a parous cervical os, no obvious cervical laceration, no visible retained products at the os. Estimated blood loss is already 800 mL. The major haemorrhage protocol is being activated, two large-bore IV cannulae are in situ, and a group-and-hold has been sent. The most likely cause is uterine atony given the soft, poorly contracted uterus The on-call gynaecologist is en route; pharmacy has misoprostol, ergometrine and carboprost available The anaesthetic team has secured the airway adequately for now.

data_table.Examination: Soft, poorly contracted, palpable uterus midway between umbilicus and symphysis; brisk bleeding from os, no cervical laceration
data_table.Investigations: Hb pre-op 124 g/L, EBL 800 mL and ongoing; group and hold sent
data_table.Procedure: Surgical TOP at 13 weeks; no obvious tissue retained at speculum

lead_in: What is the single best initial uterotonic intervention while ongoing resuscitation continues?

options.text: IV oxytocin 5 IU slow bolus followed by oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1 L saline
options.rationale: Postabortal uterine atony is managed identically to postpartum atony: oxytocin is the first-line uterotonic, given as a slow 5 IU IV bolus followed by an infusion. Second-line agents (ergot alkaloids, carboprost, misoprostol) and tranexamic acid are added if atony persists; mechanical and surgical measures escalate from there (RANZCOG PPH; WHO PPH).

options.text: Misoprostol 800 microg per rectum as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Misoprostol is a third-line uterotonic in Australian PPH/TOP haemorrhage protocols, used when first-line agents are unavailable or contraindicated; IV oxytocin is the first-line (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV as the sole haemorrhage agent, withholding any uterotonic for now
options.rationale: TXA is a useful adjunct (1 g IV within 3 h of the onset of haemorrhage) but does not address atony; uterotonics are the primary mechanism-targeted therapy (RANZCOG PPH; WOMAN trial).

options.text: Carboprost 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Carboprost is second-line after oxytocin and ergometrine; it is contraindicated in asthma. It is not the first-line agent (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Methylergometrine 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Ergometrine/methylergometrine is the standard second-line after oxytocin and is contraindicated in hypertension and pre-eclampsia. First-line uterotonic is oxytocin (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Post-TOP atonic haemorrhage is managed in parallel with major-haemorrhage resuscitation: bimanual compression, IV oxytocin bolus then infusion as first-line uterotonic, then ergometrine and carboprost if needed, plus TXA within 3 hours. Re-examine for retained tissue and cervical/uterine trauma; escalate to balloon tamponade, embolisation or laparotomy if uncontrolled (RANZCOG TOP; RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
key_points: First-line: IV oxytocin 5 IU bolus then 40 IU/L infusion (RANZCOG PPH)
Second-line: ergometrine 250 microg IM/IV (avoid in HTN, PET) (RANZCOG PPH)
Third-line: carboprost 250 microg IM q15min, max 8 doses (avoid asthma) (RANZCOG PPH)
Backup: misoprostol 800 microg PR (RANZCOG PPH)
Adjunct: TXA 1 g IV within 3 h of onset (RANZCOG PPH; WOMAN trial)
Always re-examine for retained tissue, cervical laceration, uterine perforation (RANZCOG TOP)
explanation.pearls: After surgical TOP haemorrhage that does not respond promptly to uterotonics, suspect retained tissue or uterine perforation and arrange urgent USS and theatre re-examination (RANZCOG TOP).
